quad.classify.title: Classification and Hierarchy of Quadrilaterals
quad.classify.summary: Classify using properties in a fixed order: parallel sides, side equality pattern, and angle pattern. Then validate using hierarchy relationships so your answer is always logically consistent.
keyPoints: Never classify from appearance alone; classify from properties.
A square is both a rectangle and a rhombus.
A trapezium has exactly one pair of opposite parallel sides in this lesson convention.
A kite has two pairs of adjacent equal sides.
decisionTree: Count opposite parallel side pairs first.
Check whether all sides are equal, then whether all angles are 90 degrees.
If there is exactly one parallel pair, classify as trapezium.
If there are two adjacent equal-side pairs, consider kite.
deepDive: Classification mistakes often happen when learners jump directly to familiar names like rectangle or square without validating all constraints.
A robust method is to list three facts from the problem first, then match to the smallest class that satisfies all three facts.
Hierarchy prevents contradictions: if a shape is square, statements valid for rectangle and rhombus should also hold.

workedExample.prompt: A quadrilateral has opposite sides parallel, all sides equal, and one angle is not 90 degrees. Classify it.
steps: Two pairs of opposite sides parallel -> parallelogram family.
All sides equal -> rhombus or square candidate.
Not all right angles -> exclude square.

commonErrors: Assuming all equal-sided quadrilaterals are squares.
Confusing trapezium with kite by looking only at drawing orientation.
Ignoring hierarchy and saying square is not a rectangle.
quad.classify.quickCheck: Explain why every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle is a square.
recoveryPlan: Write three observed properties before naming the class.
Use the decision sequence instead of visual guessing.
Validate hierarchy relation after choosing the class.

sections.label: S1: Property Checklist
sections.focus: Convert the problem statement into a property checklist before classification.
details: Mark parallel-side information first.
Then mark side equality (all equal, opposite equal, adjacent equal).
Finally mark angle condition (all right, opposite equal, adjacent supplementary).
quickActions: Highlight one property phrase at a time.
Do not write a shape name until all properties are listed.
sections.microPractice: Given: opposite sides parallel + all right angles. Which class fits exactly?

sections.label: S2: Decision Sequence
sections.focus: Use a deterministic order to avoid confusion among look-alike classes.
details: Step 1: number of opposite parallel pairs.
Step 2: side-equality pattern.
Step 3: angle pattern and exclusions.
quickActions: Apply the same three-step order to every item.
Cross out classes that violate any listed property.
sections.microPractice: Given: exactly one opposite parallel pair and no all-equal-side rule. Which class is most likely?

sections.label: S3: Hierarchy and Subsets
sections.focus: Understand class inclusion to reason about always-true statements.
details: Square belongs to rectangle set and rhombus set.
Rectangle and rhombus are both subtypes of parallelogram.
Subset logic is used to evaluate true/false hierarchy questions.
quickActions: Draw a mini set map: square inside rectangle and rhombus.
Test statements with the word always by checking all members of the class.
sections.microPractice: Is the statement 'every square is a rhombus' always true? Justify in one line.

sections.label: S4: Borderline Cases
sections.focus: Separate classes that share many properties but differ by one condition.
details: Square vs rhombus differs by right-angle requirement.
Kite vs rhombus differs by adjacent-equality pattern vs all-side equality.
Visual shape tilt does not change class membership.
quickActions: State one decisive distinguishing property.
Ignore orientation and rely only on property statements.
sections.microPractice: A shape has all sides equal but one angle is 120 degrees. Classify and justify.

quad.properties.title: Core Properties and Relations
quad.properties.summary: Use invariant rules to solve quickly: quadrilateral angle sum is 360 degrees, opposite/adjacent angle relations in parallelograms, and formula setup for perimeter-based unknowns.
keyPoints: Any quadrilateral interior-angle sum is 360 degrees.
In a parallelogram, opposite angles are equal.
In a parallelogram, adjacent angles are supplementary.
decisionTree: Identify which property family applies: angle-sum, opposite-angle, adjacent-angle, or formula.
Write the property equation before substituting numbers.
Solve and validate with a second property where possible.
deepDive: Most incorrect attempts come from skipping the equation setup step and computing directly from memory.
Property-to-equation translation is the bridge between geometry language and numeric solving.
Validation should include a structure check: does the solved value keep all shape constraints valid?

workedExample.prompt: In a parallelogram, one angle is 110 degrees. Find an adjacent angle.
steps: Use adjacent-angle rule: adjacent angles sum to 180 degrees.
Check with opposite-angle property for consistency.

commonErrors: Using 360 in place of 180 for adjacent-angle questions.
Confusing opposite-angle equality with adjacent-angle supplement.
Substituting formula values before isolating unknown variable.
quad.properties.quickCheck: Which equation should you write first for a rectangle with known perimeter and one side?
recoveryPlan: Write the property sentence before writing numbers.
Convert the sentence into one equation.
After solving, verify using a second property check.

sections.label: S1: Angle Sum Rule
sections.focus: Use the invariant A + B + C + D = 360 for any quadrilateral.
details: This rule does not depend on quadrilateral type.
Apply when one or more interior angles are unknown.
Set unknowns in one equation, then solve systematically.
quickActions: Write all four angles explicitly before summing.
Keep units (degrees) visible to avoid arithmetic slips.
sections.microPractice: If three interior angles are 95, 110, and 75, find the fourth angle.

sections.label: S2: Parallelogram Angle Relations
sections.focus: Choose correctly between opposite-angle equality and adjacent-angle supplement.
details: Opposite angles are equal: A = C and B = D.
Adjacent angles are supplementary: A + B = 180.
Use diagram labeling consistently to avoid relation swaps.
quickActions: Ask first: opposite pair or adjacent pair?
Write relation symbolically before substitution.
sections.microPractice: In a parallelogram, if A = 68, find adjacent angle B and opposite angle C.

sections.label: S3: Side and Diagonal Rules
sections.focus: Connect side/diagonal facts to valid class-specific conclusions.
details: Rectangle diagonals are equal.
Rhombus diagonals are perpendicular bisectors (classical property).
Use side and diagonal properties only when class assumption is justified.
quickActions: State class assumption explicitly before using diagonal rules.
Do not transfer one class property to a different class without proof.
sections.microPractice: Which diagonal property is valid for rectangle but not for every parallelogram?

sections.label: S4: Formula Setup (Perimeter/Unknown Side)
sections.focus: Translate perimeter statements into solvable linear equations.
details: Rectangle perimeter: P = 2(l + b).
Substitute known values and isolate unknown using inverse steps.
Back-substitute to confirm perimeter matches the given value.
quickActions: Expand 2(l + b) only after substitution.
Perform a final numerical check with the original formula.
sections.microPractice: A rectangle has P = 50 and l = 16. Find b.

quad.reasoning.title: Reasoning and Verification Strategy
quad.reasoning.summary: High-quality answers come from chain reasoning: identify rule, build equation, solve, and verify against the shape's constraints and hierarchy.
keyPoints: State the governing rule before doing arithmetic.
Use one-line equations that directly match the rule.
Verify with a second condition whenever possible.
Always/never/sometimes questions require class-level logic, not examples only.
decisionTree: Translate the question into 'what must be true'.
Select one geometry rule that enforces that truth.
Compute and then test consistency with class constraints.
For hierarchy questions, use subset logic.
deepDive: Reasoning errors usually come from skipping justification and jumping to numerical manipulation.
A compact explanation line improves correctness and helps detect wrong-rule usage early.
For mixed verbal problems, identify whether the bottleneck is classification, property relation, or equation setup.

workedExample.prompt: Is the statement 'every square is a rhombus' always true?
steps: Define rhombus condition: all four sides equal.
Define square condition: all four sides equal and all angles right.
Since square includes all-side-equal condition, every square satisfies rhombus definition.

commonErrors: Using one drawing as proof for always/never statements.
Giving a numeric result without stating the rule used.
Failing to test whether final answer satisfies original conditions.
quad.reasoning.quickCheck: Write a 3-step solution frame for any quadrilateral property problem.
recoveryPlan: Write one justification line before calculations.
Mark whether the question asks numeric value or truth classification.

sections.label: S1: Rule to Equation
sections.focus: Convert geometric statements into explicit equations.
details: Identify keywords: opposite, adjacent, supplementary, equal.
Map keywords to one standard equation.
Solve only after equation matches the stated relation.
quickActions: Underline relation words in the prompt.
Write the relation in symbols before substituting values.
sections.microPractice: Convert 'adjacent angles in a parallelogram are supplementary' into an equation using x.

sections.label: S2: Justify Classification Claims
sections.focus: Support class claims with definition-based evidence, not appearance.
details: A claim is valid only if all required properties are satisfied.
For always-true claims, reason over the full class set.
Use hierarchy map language: subset, special case, general case.
quickActions: Cite the exact defining property in your explanation.
Use 'therefore' only after property match is explicit.
sections.microPractice: Justify in one line: why every square is also a rectangle.

sections.label: S3: Sanity Checks
sections.focus: Validate whether computed values and claims remain geometrically consistent.
details: Check angle bounds and sum constraints.
Check class constraints still hold after solving unknowns.
Reject answers that break invariant properties.
quickActions: Re-substitute your result into the original equation.
Confirm with at least one secondary property.
sections.microPractice: If you found an adjacent angle as 195 degrees in a parallelogram, what should you conclude and why?

sections.label: S4: Multi-step Strategy
sections.focus: Handle mixed verbal + numeric questions with a stable sequence.
details: Step 1: extract givens and unknowns.
Step 2: choose the governing property.
Step 3: write/solve equation, then verify.
quickActions: Create a mini plan before calculating.
Do not combine two unrelated rules in one step.
sections.microPractice: Outline three steps to solve: perimeter known, one side known, find the other side in a rectangle.
